title: Hyponatraemia — Workup
oneLiner: Low serum sodium (<135 mmol/L) from water excess or sodium loss; common in hospitalised patients; can cause life-threatening cerebral oedema or osmotic demyelination if mismanaged.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette**  
A 74-year-old woman is admitted from a nursing home with a 2-day history of confusion and lethargy. She takes bendroflumethiazide for hypertension and recently started citalopram for depression. On examination, she is euvolemic (normal skin turgor, no oedema), BP 130/70 lying, no postural drop, mucous membranes moist. Her GCS is 14 (E4 V4 M6). Bloods show Na 122 mmol/L, K 3.8, urea 5.2, creatinine 70. Serum osmolality is 252 mOsm/kg. Urine osmolality is 412 mOsm/kg and urine sodium is 48 mmol/L.

**Pattern Clue Dissection**  
- **Elderly on thiazide + SSRI** → two drugs that impair renal water excretion (thiazide reduces diluting ability; SSRI stimulates ADH) → SIADH-like picture with low solute intake.  
- **Euvolemic on exam** → points away from hypovolaemia (dehydration) and hypervolaemia (oedema states).  
- **Low serum osmolality (<275)** → confirms true hypotonic hyponatraemia (not pseudohyponatraemia from hyperlipidaemia/paraproteinaemia).  
- **Urine osmolality >100 (here 412)** → urine not maximally dilute — indicates inappropriate ADH activity (SIADH, thiazide, or cortisol/thyroid deficiency).  
- **Urine sodium >30 (here 48)** in euvolemic/elevated ADH state → supports SIADH or ongoing sodium loss (e.g., diuretic effect).  

**Atypical Presentations That Might Trick You**  
- **Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia** – Patient looks dry (tachycardic, postural BP drop, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes). Urine Na <20 suggests extrarenal loss (vomiting, diarrhoea); urine Na >20 suggests renal loss (thiazides, adrenal insufficiency).  
- **Hypervolaemic hyponatraemia** – Patient has pitting oedema, raised JVP, ascites (heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome). Urine Na usually <20 because of secondary hyperaldosteronism.  
- **Pseudohyponatraemia** – Measured Na low but serum osmolality normal (285-295). Think of severe hypertriglyceridaemia, paraproteinaemia (multiple myeloma). No symptoms of hyponatraemia.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition**  
Hyponatraemia means the sodium concentration in your blood is lower than normal (<135 mmol/L). Because sodium is the main extracellular osmole, low sodium usually reflects an excess of water relative to sodium – either too much water retained (dilutional) or too much sodium lost (depletional). It is the most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalised patients.

**Why it matters clinically**  
- **Frequency:** Up to 30% of inpatients have mild hyponatraemia; 1-2% have severe (<125).  
- **Stakes:** Acute/severe hyponatraemia can cause brain swelling → herniation; chronic hyponatraemia corrected too rapidly causes osmotic demyelination (central pontine myelinolysis) – a catastrophic, often permanent neurological injury.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Misclassifying volume status leads to wrong treatment; misinterpreting urine osmolality/sodium; failing to check for adrenal/thyroid causes before labelling “SIADH”; overcorrecting Na inadvertently.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story**  
Sodium is controlled by thirst, ADH (vasopressin), and the kidneys. Normally, when serum sodium falls (osmolality <285), ADH secretion is suppressed, allowing the kidney to excrete dilute urine (urine osm <100, ideally <50). Hyponatraemia develops when this defence fails – either ADH is inappropriately secreted despite low osmolality (SIADH), or the kidney cannot excrete dilute urine (thiazides, renal failure), or water intake overwhelms excretion (primary polydipsia – rare if normal kidneys).

**Mechanism → sign**  
- **Brain cell swelling → neurological symptoms:** As extracellular osmolality falls, water moves into brain cells causing oedema. The skull limits expansion, so pressure rises → confusion, lethargy, headache, seizures, coma. The younger the patient and the faster the fall, the worse the symptoms (acute hyponatraemia over <48h).  
- **Euvolemia in SIADH:** ADH causes water retention (concentrated urine) but also a mild natriuresis because of volume expansion stimulating ANP → net balance of total body water up, total body sodium down slightly → clinically normal fluid status.  
- **Low serum osmolality + high urine osmolality:** This paradox is the hallmark of ADH excess (inappropriate or appropriate). An osmolality gap is absent because there is no unmeasured osmole (e.g., ethanol, mannitol, glucose).  
- **Urine sodium >30 in SIADH:** Because secondary hyperaldosteronism is suppressed by volume expansion, and ANP increases sodium excretion.

**Connecting to pattern clues**  
The vignette patient has euvolemic, hypotonic hyponatraemia with urine not maximally dilute and urine Na >30. Pathophysiologically, thiazide diuretic reduces diluting ability in the distal tubule (sodium reabsorption without water – impairs free water clearance) and SSRI stimulates ADH. Inadequate solute intake (common in elderly) further reduces water excretion. So the final common pathway is “impaired water excretion” – the kidney cannot produce a dilute enough urine even though the body is hypotonic.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
**Always first step: classify by volume status (hypo-, eu-, hypervolaemic)** | Volume Status | Common Causes (Stem Clue) | Urine Na Pattern | |---------------|---------------------------|------------------| | **Hypovolaemic** | Vomiting, diarrhoea, burns (urine Na <20 – extrarenal loss)
Diuretics (especially thiazides), adrenal insufficiency, cerebral salt wasting (urine Na >20 – renal loss) | Low in extrarenal; high in renal | | **Euvolemic** (most common) | SIADH (lung cancer, CNS infection, drugs – SSRIs, carbamazepine, NSAIDs; post-op pain; pneumonia)
Cortisol deficiency (secondary AI – check ACTH/cortisol)
Hypothyroidism (severe myxoedema)
Primary polydipsia (urine osm <100 – dilute) | Usually >30, except polydipsia where urine osm <100 | | **Hypervolaemic** | Heart failure (JVP↑, oedema)
Cirrhosis (ascites, spider naevi)
Nephrotic syndrome (periorbital oedema, proteinuria)
ESRF (oliguria, uraemia) | <20 (except in nephrotic syndrome can be variable) | **Most common cause overall:** **Euvolemic hyponatraemia due to SIADH** (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion). **Stem clues for SIADH** – small cell lung cancer (smoker, weight loss, hilar mass), post-operative (pain, nausea, opioids), meningitis/pneumonia (fever, cough, neck stiffness), drugs (SSRI, carbamazepine, MDMA). Also “beer potomania” (low solute intake) – very low urine solutes; check urine urea. **Hypovolaemic clue:** History of vomiting, diarrhoea, sweating, or high-output stoma. **Hypervolaemic clue:** Bilateral leg oedema, raised JVP, crackles, ascites. ---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria**  
Serum sodium <135 mmol/L. Classification:  
- Mild: 130–134  
- Moderate: 125–129  
- Severe: <125 (some define <120)

**Step 1: Confirm true hyponatraemia**  
- **Serum osmolality** – If **<275 mOsm/kg** → true hypotonic hyponatraemia.  
  - If normal (285–295) → pseudohyponatraemia (check lipids, protein).  
  - If elevated (>295) → hyponatraemia from hyperglycaemia (corrected Na = measured Na + 1.6 × (glucose - 5.6)/5.6) or mannitol.

**Step 2: If hypotonic, assess volume status (clinical exam) and get urine chemistries**  
- **Urine osmolality**  
  - **<100 mOsm/kg** → appropriately dilute urine. Causes: primary polydipsia, beer potomania, low solute intake. ADH fully suppressed.  
  - **>100 mOsm/kg** (often >300) → inappropriate ADH activity (SIADH, diuretics, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, hypervolaemic states).  
- **Urine sodium (spot or 24h)**  
  - **<20 mmol/L** → low Na intake, extrarenal losses (vomiting, diarrhoea), hypervolaemic states (HF, cirrhosis).  
  - **>30 mmol/L** (often >40) → renal sodium wasting (diuretics, cerebral salt wasting, adrenal insufficiency, SIADH).  

**Pattern-recognition single finding**  
- **Low serum osmolality + urine osm >100 =** inappropriate ADH (needs further workup for SIADH, adrenal, thyroid).  
- **Low serum osmolality + urine osm <100 =** primary polydipsia or low solute intake.  
- **Serum osmolality normal =** pseudohyponatraemia.

**Additional tests to clarify aetiology**  
- **Serum potassium, urea, creatinine, glucose** – hypokalaemia suggests diuretics; high urea/creatinine may indicate volume depletion; hyperglycaemia → pseudohyponatraemia.  
- **Cortisol (8am) and ACTH / short Synacthen test** – to rule out adrenal insufficiency (especially if pigmentation, shock, or hyperkalaemia).  
- **TSH** – if profound hypothyroidism suspected (rare cause).  
- **Plasma urate** – low in SIADH (due to volume expansion increasing urate clearance); normal/elevated in hypovolaemic hyponatraemia.  
- **Chest X-ray / CT chest** – to look for lung cancer (SIADH).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – only for SEVERE symptoms**  
- **Indications:** Seizures, GCS <8, coma, severe confusion/obtundation.  
- **Action:** Give **3% hypertonic saline 100 mL IV over 10-20 minutes** (can repeat once after reassessment if symptoms persist and Na has not risen by 5 mmol/L).  
- **Why:** Rapidly raises serum Na to reduce cerebral oedema – 100 mL raises Na by roughly 2-3 mmol/L in a 70kg adult. Do NOT aim for normal Na; aim for relief of severe symptoms (usually 4-6 mmol/L rise is enough).  
- **Monitor:** Check Na 1 hour after bolus. Maximum safe rise in first 24h: 8 mmol/L (10 in high-risk patients).  
- **What NOT to do:** Do NOT give normal saline (0.9%) to a euvolemic/hypervolemic patient – it provides free water relative to the patient’s low tonicity and worsens hyponatraemia. Also avoid fluid restriction during acute care.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – tailored to volume status**  
- **Hypovolaemic (loss of Na + water):** Give **0.9% saline** (isotonic, Na 154) to restore volume. Once volume restored, ADH falls, kidney excretes water, Na rises. Monitor Na every 6h.  
- **Euvolemic (SIADH):** **Fluid restriction** to 800-1000 mL/day; consider **tolvaptan** (vasopressin V2 antagonist) if severe or resistant – start 7.5-15 mg PO daily, but only under specialist guidance because of risk of overcorrection.  
- **Hypervolaemic (oedema states):** Treat underlying heart failure, cirrhosis. Fluid restrict, loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide 40 mg IV/PO). Na usually corrects as oedema resolves.  
- **Adrenal insufficiency:** Give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus then 50 mg IV Q6H** or equivalent stress dose.  
- **Hypothyroidism:** Thyroid replacement (levothyroxine) – slow correction.

**Long-term**  
- **SIADH:** Treat underlying cause. Consider **demeclocycline** 300-600 mg BID (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus-like effect) if chronic, but monitor for nephrotoxicity. Fluid restriction often lifelong.  
- **Hypovolaemic:** Stop offending diuretic, replace sodium orally (e.g., salt tablets 1-2 g TDS).  
- **Hypervolaemic:** Optimise heart failure/cirrhosis management; avoid thiazides.

**Common pitfalls**  
- **Overcorrection** – Na rising >10 mmol/L in first 24h risks osmotic demyelination. If this occurs, **desmopressin (DDAVP)** 2 µg IV can be given to slow the rise (reverses the water diuresis). Then give 5% dextrose 3-5 mL/kg to lower Na back.  
- **Giving normal saline to SIADH** – useless (the excess sodium is excreted, water retained).  
- **Not checking cortisol before calling SIADH** – adrenal insufficiency mimics SIADH and is deadly if missed.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Hypotonic Hyponatraemia (true) | Pseudohyponatraemia | Hyperglycaemic Hyponatraemia | Primary Polydipsia |
|---------|-------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Serum osmolality | <275 | 285–295 (normal) | >295 (elevated due to glucose) | <275 (low) |
| Urine osmolality | Variable (low in polydipsia, high in SIADH) | Normal urine osm | High (> serum osm) | <100 |
| Urine sodium | Variable | Variable | Variable | <20 (dilute urine) |
| Volume status | Hypo/eu/hyper | Normal | Hyper (if glucose high) | Euvolemic |
| Key clinical clue | Symptoms of low Na | Triglycerides/paraprotein | History of diabetes, thirst, polyuria | Psychogenic polydipsia; no ADH stimulation |
| Treatment | Correct volume/ADH cause | No treatment for Na | Insulin, correct glucose | Water restriction, psychiatric care |
| Risk of rapid correction | Yes – osmotic demyelination | No | Yes (if Na corrects as glucose drops) | Rare |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Cerebral oedema (acute hyponatraemia)**  
- **Presentation:** Within hours of rapid Na drop → headache, nausea, vomiting, confusion, seizures, brainstem herniation (Cushing’s: bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing).  
- **Key signs:** GCS drop, pupillary dilation, posturing.  
- **Action:** 3% hypertonic saline 100 mL bolus immediately, call ITU, imaging (CT head) to rule out other causes.

**Osmotic demyelination syndrome (chronic hyponatraemia corrected too fast)**  
- **Presentation:** 2-7 days after rapid Na correction (>10 mmol/L in 24h) – pseudobulbar palsy (dysarthria, dysphagia), quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome, ataxia, behavioural changes.  
- **Key signs:** Bilateral pontine signs (CN VI, VII, horizontal gaze palsy), altered mental state. MRI brain shows pontine hyperintensity (T2/FLAIR).  
- **Action:** If suspected, **STOP overcorrection** – give DDAVP 2 µg IV plus 5% dextrose 3 mL/kg to drop Na by 1-2 mmol/L. Consult neurology. Prevention is key: avoid correcting Na >8 mmol/L/24h (10 in high-risk: alcoholics, malnourished, liver disease, hypokalaemia).

**Other complications**  
- **Falls and fractures** – even mild hyponatraemia impairs gait and cognition, increasing injury risk.  
- **Increased length of stay** – mismanagement prolongs hospitalisation.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**  
- **SIADH** – Sort Inpatient Aetiology by Diagnostic Help:  
  - **S**mall cell lung cancer (ectopic ADH)  
  - **I**nfection (pneumonia, TB, meningitis)  
  - **A**cute brain (tumour, haemorrhage, meningitis)  
  - **D**rugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, opioids, MDMA)  
  - **H**ospital (post-op pain, nausea, volume depletion, pleural effusion)  
- **HYPONATREMIA** (for workup steps):  
  - History  
  - Volume status (hypo, eu, hyper)  
  - Osmolality (serum)  
  - Urine osmolality and sodium  
  - ADH suppressibility?  
  - Treat cause, avoid overcorrection  
  - Re-evaluate daily  
  - Endocrine (cortisol, TSH)  
  - Monitor Na 6-hourly  

**Clinical Pearls**  
1. **Always check serum osmolality first** – if it’s normal, you’re not dealing with real hyponatraemia.  
2. **Urine osm >100 in a hypotonic patient = ADH is inappropriately on** – don’t forget to check cortisol before labelling SIADH.  
3. **In hypovolaemic hyponatraemia, give normal saline** – but once volume is corrected, the ADH will drop and Na may rise rapidly, so watch the rate.  
4. **Hypertonic saline in chronic hyponatraemia is only for severe symptoms** – otherwise you risk CPM.  
5. **Thiazide-induced hyponatraemia** – tends to occur in elderly women, often with low solute intake. Stop the diuretic; Na usually corrects with fluid restriction and dietary salt.  
6. **Beer potomania** – low solute intake + high water intake → profound hyponatraemia. Urine Na <10, urine osm <100. Give solute (e.g., oral protein) and slow correction.  
7. **Serum potassium matters** – hypokalaemia stimulates ADH; correcting K can help correct Na.

**Common exam/ward mistakes**  
- Starting fluid restriction without checking urine osm – if urine is dilute, restriction is unnecessary.  
- Ordering 0.9% saline for SIADH – it’s a waste and may worsen Na if patient is euvolemic.  
- Failing to recheck Na every 4-6 hours during active correction – overcorrection is silent.  
- Labeling “SIADH” without excluding adrenal insufficiency (check 9am cortisol +/- SST) – in a stressed patient, adrenal failure can be fatal.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant)**  
- Na <120 mmol/L (severe)  
- Na <125 with neurological symptoms (confusion, ataxia, seizure)  
- Any seizure, new focal neurology, GCS <14  
- Suspected osmotic demyelination (worsening neurology 2-7 days after correction)  
- Need for hypertonic saline (3%) – must be on HDU/ICU  
- Failed fluid restriction (Na continues dropping or <125)  
- Unclear volume status or suspected adrenal insufficiency  
- Requirement for tolvaptan or demeclocycline – senior decision

**When to call ICU / crash team**  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (nursing escalation protocol)  
- **GCS < 8** or dropping rapidly  
- **Seizures** (active or post-ictal with reduced GCS)  
- **Signs of raised ICP** (Cushing’s reflex, unequal pupils)  
- **Respiratory arrest** or threatened airway  
- **Lactate > 4** (if sepsis/hypoperfusion contributing)  
- **Need for continuous hypertonic saline infusion or central line** – ICU only

**Thresholds for ICU referral**  
- Na <115 with any neurological compromise  
- Ongoing seizures despite initial 100 mL 3% saline  
- Significant overcorrection (>12 mmol/L in 24h) or planned active re-lowering

**Documentation tips**  
- Write indication for hypertonic saline, target rise (max 8 mmol/L/24h), monitoring plan.  
- Record volume status (euvolemic, hypovolemic, hypervolemic) and why.  
- Document senior discussion and review time.  

*End of note – you now have a structured, pattern-based approach to hyponatraemia workup. Use this mental scaffold every time the lab reports a low sodium.*

Monitor Na every 6h. - **Euvolemic (SIADH):** **Fluid restriction** to 800-1000 mL/day; consider **tolvaptan** (vasopressin V2 antagonist) if severe or resistant – start 7.5-15 mg PO daily, but only under specialist guidance because of risk of overcorrection. - **Hypervolaemic (oedema states):** Treat underlying heart failure, cirrhosis. Fluid restrict, loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide 40 mg IV/PO). Na usually corrects as oedema resolves. - **Adrenal insufficiency:** Give **hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus then 50 mg IV Q6H** or equivalent stress dose. - **Hypothyroidism:** Thyroid replacement (levothyroxine) – slow correction. **Long-term** - **SIADH:** Treat underlying cause. Consider **demeclocycline** 300-600 mg BID (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus-like effect) if chronic, but monitor for nephrotoxicity. Fluid restriction often lifelong. - **Hypovolaemic:** Stop offending diuretic, replace sodium orally (e.g., salt tablets 1-2 g TDS). - **Hypervolaemic:** Optimise heart failure/cirrhosis management; avoid thiazides. **Common pitfalls** - **Overcorrection** – Na rising >10 mmol/L in first 24h risks osmotic demyelination. If this occurs, **desmopressin (DDAVP)** 2 µg IV can be given to slow the rise (reverses the water diuresis). Then give 5% dextrose 3-5 mL/kg to lower Na back. - **Giving normal saline to SIADH** – useless (the excess sodium is excreted, water retained). - **Not checking cortisol before calling SIADH** – adrenal insufficiency mimics SIADH and is deadly if missed. --- ### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table | Feature | Hypotonic Hyponatraemia (true) | Pseudohyponatraemia | Hyperglycaemic Hyponatraemia | Primary Polydipsia | |---------|-------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|---------------------| | Serum osmolality | <275 | 285–295 (normal) | >295 (elevated due to glucose) | <275 (low) | | Urine osmolality | Variable (low in polydipsia, high in SIADH) | Normal urine osm | High (> serum osm) | <100 | | Urine sodium | Variable | Variable | Variable | <20 (dilute urine) | | Volume status | Hypo/eu/hyper | Normal | Hyper (if glucose high) | Euvolemic | | Key clinical clue | Symptoms of low Na | Triglycerides/paraprotein | History of diabetes, thirst, polyuria | Psychogenic polydipsia; no ADH stimulation | | Treatment | Correct volume/ADH cause | No treatment for Na | Insulin, correct glucose | Water restriction, psychiatric care | | Risk of rapid correction | Yes – osmotic demyelination | No | Yes (if Na corrects as glucose drops) | Rare | --- ### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It? **Cerebral oedema (acute hyponatraemia)** - **Presentation:** Within hours of rapid Na drop → headache, nausea, vomiting, confusion, seizures, brainstem herniation (Cushing’s: bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing). - **Key signs:** GCS drop, pupillary dilation, posturing. - **Action:** 3% hypertonic saline 100 mL bolus immediately, call ITU, imaging (CT head) to rule out other causes. **Osmotic demyelination syndrome (chronic hyponatraemia corrected too fast)** - **Presentation:** 2-7 days after rapid Na correction (>10 mmol/L in 24h) – pseudobulbar palsy (dysarthria, dysphagia), quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome, ataxia, behavioural changes. - **Key signs:** Bilateral pontine signs (CN VI, VII, horizontal gaze palsy), altered mental state. MRI brain shows pontine hyperintensity (T2/FLAIR). - **Action:** If suspected, **STOP overcorrection** – give DDAVP 2 µg IV plus 5% dextrose 3 mL/kg to drop Na by 1-2 mmol/L. Consult neurology. Prevention is key: avoid correcting Na >8 mmol/L/24h (10 in high-risk: alcoholics, malnourished, liver disease, hypokalaemia). **Other complications** - **Falls and fractures** – even mild hyponatraemia impairs gait and cognition, increasing injury risk. - **Increased length of stay** – mismanagement prolongs hospitalisation. --- ### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls **Mnemonics** - **SIADH** – Sort Inpatient Aetiology by Diagnostic Help: - **S**mall cell lung cancer (ectopic ADH) - **I**nfection (pneumonia, TB, meningitis) - **A**cute brain (tumour, haemorrhage, meningitis) - **D**rugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, opioids, MDMA) - **H**ospital (post-op pain, nausea, volume depletion, pleural effusion) - **HYPONATREMIA** (for workup steps): - History - Volume status (hypo, eu, hyper) - Osmolality (serum) - Urine osmolality and sodium - ADH suppressibility? - Treat cause, avoid overcorrection - Re-evaluate daily - Endocrine (cortisol, TSH) - Monitor Na 6-hourly **Clinical Pearls** 1. **Always check serum osmolality first** – if it’s normal, you’re not dealing with real hyponatraemia. 2. **Urine osm >100 in a hypotonic patient = ADH is inappropriately on** – don’t forget to check cortisol before labelling SIADH. 3. **In hypovolaemic hyponatraemia, give normal saline** – but once volume is corrected, the ADH will drop and Na may rise rapidly, so watch the rate. 4. **Hypertonic saline in chronic hyponatraemia is only for severe symptoms** – otherwise you risk CPM. 5. **Thiazide-induced hyponatraemia** – tends to occur in elderly women, often with low solute intake. Stop the diuretic; Na usually corrects with fluid restriction and dietary salt. 6. **Beer potomania** – low solute intake + high water intake → profound hyponatraemia. Urine Na <10, urine osm <100. Give solute (e.g., oral protein) and slow correction. 7. **Serum potassium matters** – hypokalaemia stimulates ADH; correcting K can help correct Na. **Common exam/ward mistakes** - Starting fluid restriction without checking urine osm – if urine is dilute, restriction is unnecessary. - Ordering 0.9% saline for SIADH – it’s a waste and may worsen Na if patient is euvolemic. - Failing to recheck Na every 4-6 hours during active correction – overcorrection is silent. - Labeling “SIADH” without excluding adrenal insufficiency (check 9am cortisol +/- SST) – in a stressed patient, adrenal failure can be fatal. --- ### 10. Escalation Criteria **When to call your senior (registrar/consultant)** - Na <120 mmol/L (severe) - Na <125 with neurological symptoms (confusion, ataxia, seizure) - Any seizure, new focal neurology, GCS <14 - Suspected osmotic demyelination (worsening neurology 2-7 days after correction) - Need for hypertonic saline (3%) – must be on HDU/ICU - Failed fluid restriction (Na continues dropping or <125) - Unclear volume status or suspected adrenal insufficiency - Requirement for tolvaptan or demeclocycline – senior decision **When to call ICU / crash team** - **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (nursing escalation protocol) - **GCS < 8** or dropping rapidly - **Seizures** (active or post-ictal with reduced GCS) - **Signs of raised ICP** (Cushing’s reflex, unequal pupils) - **Respiratory arrest** or threatened airway - **Lactate > 4** (if sepsis/hypoperfusion contributing) - **Need for continuous hypertonic saline infusion or central line** – ICU only **Thresholds for ICU referral** - Na <115 with any neurological compromise - Ongoing seizures despite initial 100 mL 3% saline - Significant overcorrection (>12 mmol/L in 24h) or planned active re-lowering **Documentation tips** - Write indication for hypertonic saline, target rise (max 8 mmol/L/24h), monitoring plan. - Record volume status (euvolemic, hypovolemic, hypervolemic) and why. - Document senior discussion and review time. *End of note – you now have a structured, pattern-based approach to hyponatraemia workup. Use this mental scaffold every time the lab reports a low sodium.*
